coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent imaging and toxicology study of alga-derived carbon dots in zebrafish
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yue
Gu, Zhizhi
Dong, Jingyi
Zhu, Jie
Liu, Cunguang

Lu, Meichen

Cao, Shengnan
Wang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: With the widespread application of carbon dots (CDs) in fluorescence imaging, their toxicity has become a focal point of concern. The potential toxicity of CDs synthesized from different raw materials remains an unresolved issue. Laver and wakame, which are commonly popular sea vegetable foods rich in nutrients, were utilized to investigate whether synthetic CDs derived from these alga sources retain medicinal value. Herein, two types of fluorescent alga-derived CDs were prepared through hydrothermal synthesis using laver and wakame respectively. Zebrafish were immersed in both types of CDs to observe their fluorescence imaging effects within the zebrafish bodies. It was observed that laver-derived CDs and wakame-derived CDs exhibited similar luminescence properties but differed in terms of fish egg imaging localization. Additionally, intestinal flora sequencing revealed varying degrees of influence on the zebrafish gut microbiota by the two types of CDs, suggesting that both alga-derived CDs could enhance the abundance of intestinal flora in zebrafish.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Intestinal flora
Fluorescence imaging
Laver
Wakame

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of fluorescence imaging and toxicology study of laver-derived CDs and wakame-derived CDs in zebrafish.

(a) TEM of L-CDs; (b) the corresponding particle size distribution map of L-CDs; (c) TEM of W-CDs; (d) the corresponding particle size distribution map of W-CDs; (e) XRD patterns of L-CDs and W-CDs; (f) FTIR spectra of L-CDs and W-CDs.
(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs; (b) Fluorescence spectrum of L-CDs; (c) Fluorescence spectrum of W-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectra of three CDs and deconvoluted C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) spectra of CDs.
Metabolic processes of CDs in zebrafish from 5 hpf to 96 hpf in CDs solution (0.5 mg/mL). Bright field (a, c, e) and fluorescent field (b, d, f). Scale bars, 250 μm.
Metabolic processes of CDs in zebrafish. Bright-field (a) and fluorescent field (b). Scale bars, 250 μm.
Longitudinal section scan image of zebrafish (a) control group (b) L-CDs (c) W-CDs.
Intestinal flora abundance map of zebrafish.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent imaging and toxicology study of alga-derived carbon dots in zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Applied Biology and Aquaculture of Northern Fishes in Liaoning Province, Dalian Ocean University, Dalian 116023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Applied Biology and Aquaculture of Northern Fishes in Liaoning Province, Dalian Ocean University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Applied Biology and Aquaculture of Northern Fishes in Liaoning Province, Dalian Ocean University, Dalian, 116023, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the widespread application of carbon dots (CDs) in fluorescence imaging, their toxicity has become a focal point of concern. The potential toxicity of CDs synthesized from different raw materials remains an unresolved issue. Laver and wakame, which are commonly popular sea vegetable foods rich in nutrients, were utilized to investigate whether synthetic CDs derived from these alga sources retain medicinal value. Herein, two types of fluorescent alga-derived CDs were prepared through hydrothermal synthesis using laver and wakame respectively. Zebrafish were immersed in both types of CDs to observe their fluorescence imaging effects within the zebrafish bodies. It was observed that laver-derived CDs and wakame-derived CDs exhibited similar luminescence properties but differed in terms of fish egg imaging localization. Additionally, intestinal flora sequencing revealed varying degrees of influence on the zebrafish gut microbiota by the two types of CDs, suggesting that both alga-derived CDs could enhance the abundance of intestinal flora in zebrafish.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The ocean is a treasure house of resources. Alga, fish, shellfish and other organisms can be used not only as human food but also as excellent carbon sources. Alga also contains N, S and other elements, which can be used as excellent hybrid factors in experiments. At present, carrageenan, algae polysaccharides, fucoidans and other carbon spots in common edible alga are used in the detection, biological imaging and other aspects (Godavarthi et al., 2017; Li et al., 2021a, 2021b; Liu et al., 2023; Mao et al., 2023; Tang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2019; Yue et al., 2022). Laver is a common edible alga in coastal cities of China (Cho and Rhee, 2019), which is purple in seawater. Coastal people often store it in the sun. After drying, it becomes dark purple, hence its name. The vegetable extract has anti-metastasis properties in inhibiting the adhesion, invasion and migration of SK-Hep1 human hepatoma cells (Do Thi and Hwang, 2014). The active substances isolated from porphyra also have the effect of inhibiting fat (Choi et al., 2022). Wakame is another common edible alga. Both algae contain chlorophyll, protein, unsaturated fatty acids and so on. According to previous reports, wakame and wakame extract have the functions of anti-oxidation, anti-inflammation and promoting wound healing (Phull et al., 2017; Ferreira et al., 2021; He et al., 2022; Taboada et al., 2013).

Carbon dots (CDs) are tiny carbon particles that emit fluorescence and are used for biological imaging due to their excellent biocompatibility. Their properties can vary depending on the precursor used to synthesize them. Under normal circumstances, due to the large particle size of algae, it is not easy to disperse and dissolve, and the effect is difficult to give full play. After algae are prepared into fluorescent CDs, as the nanoscale material (particle size less than 20 nm), not only maintains a series of excellent characteristics similar to other CDs in size, green, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, etc., but also carries some characteristics of algae itself. However, it is essential to conduct necessary biological experiments before using CDs (Dias et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2022). Currently, most CDs synthesized from biomass precursors have similar luminescent characteristics and low toxicity (Ahmadian-Fard-Fini et al., 2018; Selvaraju et al., 2022; Gudimella et al., 2021; Zulfajri et al., 2019). Their effects on organisms must be carefully observed to ensure their safe use.

It is important to study the effects of CDs on intestinal health to assess their safety, as they may eventually come in contact with the intestinal tract. Some researchers have used Fuligo Plantae to generate CDs and analyze their impact on the intestinal flora (Zhao et al., 2023). Studies have shown that CDs made from Fuligo Plantae, and other Chinese herbal medicines, have a significant inhibitory effect on alcoholic gastric ulcers in rats (Lu et al., 2021). The intestinal microbiome is crucial for human health, and exposure to CDs can affect this microbiome by altering its composition, diversity, and function, leading to various intestinal diseases and pathological changes (Bantun et al., 2022). Studies have shown that CDs may cause pathological damage to the intestinal tract by inhibiting the growth of probiotics, activating intestinal inflammation, decreasing the abundance of beneficial bacteria, increasing the abundance of harmful bacteria, and increasing the ratio of Chlamydia to Bacteroides (Jia et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). It is important to note that the effects of CDs may vary depending on their source. Edible algae is safe for use as feed in aquaculture and is free of pollutants. Zebrafish eggs were imaged using laver-derived CDs and wakame-derived CDs to monitor their development and metabolism, to study the effects of CDs on zebrafish intestines, and to predict their application in vivo bioimaging ( Fig. 1). In the field of biofluorescent materials, the use of natural alga to prepare CDs is safer and more controllable than conventional dyes and genetic modification.

CDs were prepared from distilled water (50 mL) by solvothermal treatment in two algae (10 g) at 200 °C 8 h in a reactor. The CDs prepared by Laver and Wakame, are named L-CDs and W-CDs, respectively. In detail, the obtained dark brown solutions after solvothermal treatment by Laver and Wakame which were collected by suction filtrating using a pinhole filter (0.22 μm) were to obtain L-CDs and W-CDs. The precipitates were freeze-dried to obtain the brown product of L-CDs and W-CDs, respectively.

Female and male zebrafish were separated (Shanghai Feixi Co., Ltd.), fed 3 times a day and changed water once, temporarily cultured for 7 days in 12 h light period, male and female fish were placed at both ends of the spawning box respectively, both ends was separated by the transparent plate which was removed at 8:00 a.m. the next day, eggs were collected 2 h later.

The particles of L-CDs ( Fig. 2a) and W-CDs (Fig. 2c) have good dispersibility, and all smaller than 6 nm. XRD pattern indicates the presence of graphitic carbon according to a peak at approximately 24° (Fig. 2e). FTIR analysis showed that both CDs had characteristic peaks at 3400 cm−1, 1600 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1, indicating that both CDs contained carboxyl (CO) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups. The structures of the two CDs were similar but slightly different, and both CDs had good water solubility. Moreover, the weak stretching vibration peaks of the C-O bond at 1100 cm−1, -CH2 at 2800 cm−1 and -CH3 at 2900 cm−1 can be found, revealing the presence of a small amount of two algae (Fig. 2f).

In the UV–visible absorption spectrum, L-CDs show a distinct absorption peak near 220 nm and an additional absorption peak at 330 nm; W-CDs have a distinct peak near 220 nm. Excitation-dependent fluorescence of both CDs was observed in the excitation wavelength range of 350 nm to 420 nm, with L-CDs exhibiting the strongest emission peak at 420 nm and W-CDs exhibiting the strongest emission peak at 450 nm with increasing excitation wavelength, both corresponding to blue fluorescence ( Fig. 3b-c).

To explore the chemical structures of the two samples, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted. An examination of the full XPS spectra of both samples, as displayed in Fig. 4, demonstrates the presence of C, N, and O. The C, O, and N content in L-CDs and W-CDs are similar. Upon closer inspection of the high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS spectra, it becomes evident that both samples contain C-C, C-O, C-N, and CO bonds (as observed in the C 1 s XPS spectra), C-N and N-H bonds (as observed in the N 1 s XPS spectra), as well as CO and C-O bonds (as observed in the O 1 s XPS spectra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence vivo of CDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: It is worth noting that when zebrafish were exposed to varying concentrations of CDs solutions ( Fig. 5), there were no significant differences in zebrafish mortality rates between the control group and the 0.5 mg/mL solution. As a result, this concentration was selected for further exposure in the fluorescence imaging experiment. The experiment revealed that the fluorescence intensity of zebrafish eggs increased with exposure time up to 96 hpf and that L-CDs and W-CDs accumulated in the yolk sac after hatching (Fig. 5). To investigate the metabolic time of CDs in zebrafish eggs, a blank solution was used for observation after soaking at 48hpf. After stopping exposure at 48hpf, the fluorescence intensity in fish fry decreased significantly, indicating that water-soluble CDs could not be accumulated in vivo ( Fig. 6).

We did not observe any apparent abnormalities in the fish eggs during the experiment. Furthermore, the metabolic experiment revealed that the eggs did not accumulate carbon points. This is great news for the application of CDs, as nanoparticles that cannot be accumulated may cause repeated toxic effects.

Take adult zebrafish and soak them in two CDs solutions (1 mg/mL) for 12 hours. Each group was sampled and observed by HE staining ( Fig. 7). Compared with the control group, there was no significant change in the two experimental groups, indicating that soaking CDs solution would not cause organic lesions of adult fish.

The intestinal flora abundance of zebrafish was found to be affected by CDs, as compared to the control group. Proteobacteria was found to be the most abundant (92.86%), with the L-CDs group having the highest abundance of Proteobacteria (75.56%) and Bacteroides (21.28%), and the W-CDs group having Proteobacteria (78.79%), Bacteroides (14.19%), and Firmicutes (5.11%) as their highest abundance ( Fig. 8). The CDs had a significant impact on the diversity of intestinal flora in zebrafish, with different carbon points having varying effects on different flora. While L-CDs increased bacteroidetes, they also reduced the abundance of firmicutes. On the other hand, W-CDs resulted in a lower chance of bacteroidetes, but increased the abundance of other species of bacteria.

The results of fluorescence imaging of zebrafish demonstrated that laver-derived CDs and wakame-derived CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence imaging ability in zebrafish, indicating their compatibility with biological systems. At a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, laver-derived CDs and wakame-derived CDs did not affect the survival of zebrafish adults or eggs. The results of histological observation of adult zebrafish after soaking in CDs showed that soaking in CDs solution did not cause organic lesions of adult zebrafish. Analysis of the gut flora via 16 S gene analysis showed that CDs increased diversity and improved the overall health of the gut flora. This study aimed to investigate the chronic toxicity and genotoxicity of CDs in zebrafish and provide valuable insights into their applications in biology and medicine. Analysis of CDs on zebrafish gut flora revealed that CDs increased the diversity of the gut flora and improved its overall health. This study provides valuable insights into the effects of CDs on zebrafish fluorescence imaging and fish gut flora, offering new perspectives for future nanomedicine applications by analyzing the sequencing data of zebrafish gut flora.

Zhizhi Gu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jingyi Dong: Validation, Formal analysis. Yue Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Guohan Li: Investigation. Meichen Lu: Validation. Jie Zhu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Cunguang Liu: Formal analysis. Wei Wang: Writing – review & editing. Jian Han: Conceptualization. Shengnan Cao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Liaoning Provincial Doctoral Research Start-Up Fund (No. 2020-BS-218) and Basic research projects in higher education institutions of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial (JYTZD2023038).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots from averrhoa carambola fruit extract as a fluorescent probe for methyl orange
